events.label: reasoning
events.text: We need answer JSON only. Need identify condition: efflorescence on CMU veneer after wet winter. Joints intact, weep holes open, interior humidity normal. Need determine if corrective masonry work warranted. Efflorescence is generally cosmetic, often resolves with weathering; not a structural defect. If no moisture issues, no corrective work warranted. Decision pass? Condition accepted, no flagged categories. Risk low. Component conditions empty. Findings: efflorescence present, likely soluble salts leaching, joints/weep holes OK, no moisture intrusion. Actions: monitor, dry brush/wash if aesthetic concern, verify no ongoing water source. Rationale. References: BIA Tech Notes 23 (efflorescence), maybe TMS 402/602, IBC. Need include all fields. percent_complete 100 accepted. value 0. sound_source etc default. order empty, workable empty. confidence high. event_time 0, rate 0. Let's craft JSON.

events.label: response
events.text:
{
  "decision": "pass",
  "risk": "low",
  "work_stage": "accepted",
  "component_conditions": [],
  "percent_complete": 100,
  "value": 0,
  "sound_source": "",
  "confidence": 0.9,
  "event_time": 0.0,
  "rate": 0.0,
  "order": [],
  "workable": [],
  "findings": [
    "Light white powdery haze consistent with efflorescence on CMU veneer after wet winter",
    "Mortar joints are tooled concave and intact",
    "Weep holes are open and unobstructed",
    "Interior humidity readings behind the wall are normal, indicating no significant moisture intrusion"
  ],
  "actions": [
    "Monitor the veneer through additional weathering cycles; efflorescence often diminishes naturally",
    "If aesthetics remain unacceptable after drying, lightly brush or wash per manufacturer/BIA guidance",
    "Confirm no persistent water source, defective flashing, or blocked drainage before considering masonry removal"
  ],
  "rationale": "The white powdery deposit is characteristic of efflorescence—soluble salts migrating to the masonry surface with moisture and crystallizing as it dries. With intact joints, functional weep holes, and normal interior humidity, there is no evidence of a water-management defect or structural problem. Efflorescence is typically a cosmetic, self-limiting condition, so corrective masonry work is not warranted at this time.",
  "references": [
    "BIA Technical Notes 23 - Efflorescence",
    "TMS 602/ACI 530.1 - Specification for Masonry Structures",
    "IBC Chapter 21 - Masonry"
  ]
}
